cases.title: The Antacid That Failed
cases.presentation: Mr. Ramesh Kulkarni, 58 years old, walks into casualty at 11am with his son. He has had central chest heaviness for 6 hours, which he attributed to last night's heavy dinner. He took two Gelusil tablets at home — they gave no relief. He appears diaphoretic and keeps rubbing his chest as he speaks.
cases.india_context: Acute MI in India presents a decade earlier than in Western populations, with poorly controlled diabetes and hypertension as the dominant risk factors. Misattribution to acidity or gas trouble is extremely common and causes dangerous delays in presentation to casualty.

history_cards.question: Does the pain radiate anywhere?
history_cards.answer: Yes, some discomfort going into my left arm. I thought I pulled a muscle carrying grocery bags yesterday.
history_cards.hint_value: Left arm radiation is a classic ACS feature — patient is rationalising it away, a classic anchoring trap

history_cards.question: Any history of diabetes or blood pressure?
history_cards.answer: Both. Sugar is there for 12 years, not well controlled. BP also high — I take one tablet but not regularly.
history_cards.hint_value: Poorly controlled DM + HTN = major cardiac risk factors. DM autonomic neuropathy blunts pain — explains atypical presentation

history_cards.question: Any previous heart problem or hospital admission?
history_cards.answer: No stents. But two years back my doctor said ECG had some changes. I never went for follow-up.
history_cards.hint_value: Prior ECG changes without follow-up suggests pre-existing ischaemic heart disease

history_cards.question: Did it come on suddenly or gradually?
history_cards.answer: Started slowly after dinner last night, became worse this morning. I thought sleeping would help but it did not.
history_cards.hint_value: Gradual onset over hours with worsening — consistent with evolving MI, not aortic dissection

history_cards.question: Any vomiting or sweating?
history_cards.answer: Yes, vomited once this morning. And my shirt is wet — I am sweating a lot even though it is not hot.
history_cards.hint_value: Diaphoresis and vomiting are vagal symptoms common in inferior MI — Bezold-Jarisch reflex from RCA ischaemia

history_cards.question: Any travel recently or leg swelling?
history_cards.answer: No travel. No leg swelling that I know of.
history_cards.hint_value: Screens for PE as a mimic — negative here, low yield in this context

findings.name: Heart Rate
findings.result: 58 bpm — bradycardic

findings.name: Blood Pressure
findings.result: 92/60 mmHg — hypotensive

findings.name: SpO2 and RR
findings.result: SpO2 97% on room air. RR 18/min.

findings.name: Auscultation
findings.result: S1 S2 heard. No murmurs. S4 gallop present at apex.

findings.name: JVP
findings.result: JVP elevated at 5cm above sternal angle — right heart involvement

imaging_options.report:
Rate: 58 bpm. Rhythm: Sinus bradycardia.

ST elevation of 2–3mm in leads II, III, aVF.
Reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL.
Early Q wave formation in lead III.
No ST changes in precordial leads V1–V6.

Impression: Acute inferior wall MI. Pattern consistent with RCA occlusion. Bradycardia may reflect vagal tone or early AV node involvement (RCA supplies AV node in 85% of individuals).

Heart size: Upper limit of normal (CT ratio 0.52).
Lung fields: Clear. No pulmonary oedema. No pleural effusion.
Mediastinum: Normal width.

Impression: No acute pulmonary pathology. CXR does not exclude ACS — this is a clinical and ECG diagnosis.

LV: Regional wall motion abnormality — hypokinesia of inferior and posterior walls. EF 45% (mildly reduced).
RV: Mildly dilated with reduced function — consistent with RV infarction.
Valves: Trivial MR. Aortic valve normal.
Pericardium: No effusion.

Impression: Acute inferior + right ventricular MI. RV involvement: avoid nitrates and aggressive diuresis.

optimal_path.explanation: In a middle-aged diabetic male with chest heaviness and diaphoresis, do ECG first — it gives the diagnosis immediately at the same cost as labs. Two history questions (radiation + risk factors) establish suspicion. Vitals showing bradycardia and hypotension in an MI patient is the RV involvement clue. Troponin confirms the ECG finding.

debrief.pathophysiology: Acute occlusion of the Right Coronary Artery (RCA) causes transmural ischaemia of the inferior LV wall, seen as ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF. The RCA supplies the RV and AV node in 85% of people, explaining RV infarction and bradycardia respectively. The Bezold-Jarisch reflex — increased vagal tone from inferior wall ischaemia — further causes bradycardia, hypotension, and vomiting.
debrief.classic_presentation: Middle-aged male with cardiac risk factors presenting with central or epigastric heaviness, diaphoresis, nausea/vomiting, left arm or jaw radiation. Inferior MIs often mimic GI illness — epigastric pain and vomiting are more prominent than in anterior MIs. Bradycardia and hypotension (not tachycardia) are characteristic.
key_discriminators: ST elevation in II, III, aVF with reciprocal depression in I, aVL
Bradycardia + hypotension (not tachycardia — Bezold-Jarisch reflex)
Elevated JVP with clear lungs — RV infarction triad
Critically elevated Troponin I and CK-MB
common_mimics: GERD/Gastritis — shares epigastric location and post-meal onset, but antacids provide relief, no troponin rise, no ECG changes
Aortic Dissection — tearing pain radiating to back, BP asymmetry between arms, mediastinal widening on CXR
Acute Pericarditis — diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation, PR depression, sharp positional pain worse lying flat, friction rub
debrief.treatment: Dual antiplatelet therapy: Ecosprin (Aspirin) 300mg + Clopidogrel 600mg or Ticagrelor 180mg immediately. Primary PCI within 90 minutes is gold standard — activate cath lab. If PCI unavailable within 120 minutes, fibrinolysis (Tenecteplase or Streptokinase) within 30 minutes of arrival. For RV infarction: avoid nitrates completely; give cautious IV fluids to maintain preload.
debrief.clinical_pearl: RV infarction complicates 30–50% of inferior MIs. Always do right-sided ECG leads (V3R, V4R) in any inferior MI — ST elevation in V4R confirms RV involvement. The haemodynamic triad: hypotension + elevated JVP + clear lungs = RV infarction. Giving nitrates to these patients causes catastrophic hypotension.
debrief.attendings_note:
This patient told you it was gas trouble and you almost believed him. I want you to remember this — in a 58-year-old poorly controlled diabetic male with diaphoresis, 'chest heaviness that does not respond to Gelusil' is a heart attack until the ECG proves otherwise. The word 'acidity' should have raised your suspicion, not lowered it. Diabetic patients get silent or atypical MIs because neuropathy blunts their pain. When the pain is vague and the patient is minimising it, your clinical suspicion must be loud.

Now look at his vitals carefully. Bradycardic at 58, BP only 92/60. Every student expects a heart attack patient to be tachycardic and sweaty — yes, sweaty, but not bradycardic. Inferior MIs are different. The Bezold-Jarisch reflex slows the heart down. And his elevated JVP with clear lungs tells you the right ventricle is involved. This is the empty LV syndrome — the right heart cannot fill the left heart, and if you give nitrates now you will kill him.

The correct move was ECG within 2 minutes of arrival — before bloods, before anything else. You ordered troponin first, which is understandable, but troponin takes hours to rise. In the first hour of a heart attack, the ECG is positive and the troponin can still be completely normal. Never delay the ECG for blood tests in any chest pain patient.
debrief.india_context: Acute MI in India presents a decade earlier than in Western populations — a 58-year-old here is equivalent to a 68-year-old in European studies. Misattribution to acidity or gas trouble is the single biggest cause of delayed presentation in Indian casualties. Pan masala, tobacco chewing, and bidi smoking are important additional risk factors to screen for.

related_cases.teaches: Silent MI in women and diabetics — how ischaemia presents without classic chest pain

cases.title: Drowning From the Inside
cases.presentation: Mrs. Saraswathi Venkataraman, 65 years old, is brought to casualty at 3am by her daughter. She woke up suddenly unable to breathe and has been sitting upright ever since — she says lying down makes it much worse. She has been on 'BP tablets' for many years but stopped them 2 weeks ago because she 'felt fine'. On examination she is visibly breathless and you can hear a gurgling sound from across the room.
cases.india_context: Hypertension is the leading cause of heart failure in India, and medication non-compliance is extremely common — patients often stop antihypertensives when they feel well. Acute LVF presenting at night (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea) is a classic pattern that students must recognise immediately as a cardiac emergency.

history_cards.question: Did she stop any medicines recently?
history_cards.answer: Yes, she stopped her BP tablet — Amlodipine — about 2 weeks ago. She said she was feeling fine and the tablet was causing ankle swelling.
history_cards.hint_value: Abrupt antihypertensive discontinuation causes rebound hypertension which precipitates acute LVF — this is the trigger

history_cards.question: How many pillows does she sleep on at night?
history_cards.answer: Three pillows for the last 6 months. She cannot sleep flat — she says it makes her breathless.
history_cards.hint_value: Orthopnoea (3-pillow orthopnoea) indicates elevated left atrial pressure — chronic heart failure is the background

history_cards.question: Any swelling in the legs?
history_cards.answer: Yes, both ankles swollen for the last 2–3 months. She thought it was from standing too long.
history_cards.hint_value: Bilateral pedal oedema = fluid overload, consistent with chronic heart failure now acutely decompensated

history_cards.question: Any previous heart attack or heart problem?
history_cards.answer: No heart attack she knows of. But she has been on BP medicines for 15 years. One doctor said her heart was 'a little weak' last year but she did not follow up.
history_cards.hint_value: Known hypertension for 15 years + 'weak heart' = hypertensive cardiomyopathy as underlying cause

history_cards.question: Any cough? What does it look like?
history_cards.answer: Yes, coughing a lot since last night. The sputum is pinkish — she thought she had a chest infection.
history_cards.hint_value: Pink frothy sputum is pathognomonic of pulmonary oedema — student may mistake this for pneumonia

history_cards.question: Any fever or recent illness?
history_cards.answer: No fever. No cold or cough before last night. No sick contacts.
history_cards.hint_value: Absence of fever makes infective cause less likely — helps rule out pneumonia as primary diagnosis

findings.name: Blood Pressure
findings.result: 192/110 mmHg — severely hypertensive

findings.name: Heart Rate and SpO2
findings.result: HR 112 bpm. SpO2 82% on room air — critically low.

findings.name: Respiratory Rate
findings.result: 34/min — severely tachypnoeic. Using accessory muscles.

findings.name: Auscultation — Lungs
findings.result: Bilateral fine crepitations from bases to mid-zones. Expiratory wheeze bilaterally (cardiac asthma).

findings.name: Auscultation — Heart
findings.result: S1 S2 heard. S3 gallop present. No murmurs.

findings.name: JVP
findings.result: JVP elevated at 6cm above sternal angle.

imaging_options.report:
Cardiomegaly: present (CT ratio 0.58).
Upper lobe venous diversion: present.
Bilateral perihilar haziness: 'bat-wing' pattern of pulmonary oedema.
Kerley B lines: present at bases.
Bilateral pleural effusions: small, more prominent on right.

Impression: Appearances consistent with acute pulmonary oedema. Cardiomegaly suggests underlying cardiomyopathy.

Rate: 112 bpm. Rhythm: Sinus tachycardia.
LVH criteria: Sokolow-Lyon index positive (S in V1 + R in V5 = 38mm).
ST depression: 1mm in V4–V6 (strain pattern).
No acute ST elevation.

Impression: LVH with strain pattern. No acute MI. Consistent with hypertensive heart disease.

LV: Concentric hypertrophy. EF 35% (severely reduced). Global hypokinesia.
LA: Dilated.
Valves: Mild mitral regurgitation.
IVC: Dilated, non-collapsing — elevated RA pressure.

Impression: Hypertensive cardiomyopathy with severely reduced EF. Findings consistent with acute decompensated heart failure.

optimal_path.explanation: The triad of sudden nocturnal breathlessness + orthopnoea + bilateral crepitations in a known hypertensive who stopped medications points directly to acute LVF. CXR gives the bat-wing pattern and confirms the diagnosis fast. BNP markedly elevated confirms cardiac origin. ABG shows hypoxia severity and guides oxygen therapy. ECG shows LVH + strain but no acute MI. Echo confirms EF and underlying cardiomyopathy.

debrief.pathophysiology: Abrupt discontinuation of antihypertensives caused rebound hypertension, dramatically increasing left ventricular afterload. The already hypertrophied and dysfunctional LV (from 15 years of hypertension) could not maintain forward output, causing acute backpressure into the pulmonary circulation. Fluid transudates into alveolar spaces, impairing gas exchange — producing the characteristic pink frothy sputum and bilateral crepitations.
debrief.classic_presentation: Elderly patient with known hypertension, waking suddenly from sleep unable to breathe (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea), sitting bolt upright, orthopnoeic, with pink frothy sputum, bilateral crepitations, and markedly elevated BP. Often a history of missed medications or dietary salt excess as a precipitant.
key_discriminators: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea + orthopnoea (cardiac) vs fever + productive cough (pneumonia)
Bilateral crepitations from bases upward + S3 gallop = cardiogenic
Pink frothy sputum is pathognomonic of pulmonary oedema
CXR bat-wing perihilar haziness + Kerley B lines + cardiomegaly = LVF not pneumonia
common_mimics: Pneumonia — fever, focal consolidation on CXR, productive purulent sputum, no orthopnoea
Acute severe asthma — wheeze prominent, younger patient, no cardiomegaly, normal BNP
COPD exacerbation — known smoker with barrel chest, hyperinflation on CXR, no orthopnoea or S3
debrief.treatment: Sit patient upright immediately. High-flow oxygen, target SpO2 > 94%. IV Furosemide (Lasix) 40–80mg stat — diuresis reduces preload. IV morphine 2–4mg for anxiolysis and venodilation (use cautiously). IV nitrates (GTN infusion) to reduce afterload — only if SBP > 110 mmHg. If not responding, consider BiPAP/NIV. Monitor urine output, K+, creatinine closely.
debrief.clinical_pearl: Cardiac asthma — bilateral wheeze in acute LVF — is frequently mistaken for bronchial asthma and treated with bronchodilators alone, which can be dangerous. The key differentiator is the cardiac history, orthopnoea, and CXR findings. A bedside BNP or NT-proBNP, if available, instantly separates cardiac from pulmonary dyspnoea.
debrief.attendings_note:
She stopped her Amlodipine because she felt fine — this is the most common story I hear in casualty every week. You must counsel every hypertensive patient that they will feel fine because the medicines are working, not because the disease is gone. The moment they stop, the rebound can land them in casualty gasping for breath at 3am.

Now, when you hear bilateral wheeze in a breathless patient, your first instinct may be to reach for a nebuliser. That would be a mistake here. This is cardiac asthma — the wheeze comes from peribronchial oedema in the lungs, not from bronchospasm. If you give salbutamol and miss the LVF, she will deteriorate. Always look at the full picture: elderly, hypertensive, orthopnoeic, S3 gallop, bilateral crepitations from the bases upward, pink frothy sputum. This is a drowning lung, not an asthmatic one.

The CXR here is a textbook — bat-wing shadowing, Kerley B lines, cardiomegaly, upper lobe diversion. Learn this film. It will save lives.
debrief.india_context: Hypertension is the commonest cause of heart failure in India, unlike in the West where ischaemic heart disease dominates. Medication non-compliance, driven by cost and the feeling of being 'cured,' is the most common precipitant of acute decompensation. Salt-heavy diets (pickles, papads, processed foods) are a major contributing factor in Indian patients.

cases.title: Not Just the Drinks
cases.presentation: Mr. Debabrata Ghosh, 62 years old, is brought to casualty by his wife at 9pm. His wife says he suddenly started speaking 'strangely' about 2 hours ago and his right side of the face is drooping. His son, who called the ambulance, told the triage nurse 'he has had a few drinks at a family function tonight — this is probably the alcohol.' Mr. Ghosh has known atrial fibrillation and was prescribed Warfarin six months ago but stopped it after a minor cut bled 'too much.'
cases.india_context: Stroke is the second leading cause of death in India. Atrial fibrillation without anticoagulation is a major preventable cause, yet Warfarin compliance is poor due to cost, monitoring burden, and fear of bleeding. The 'brain attack' campaign has had limited reach in non-metro India — delays in presentation beyond the thrombolysis window are very common.

history_cards.question: Exactly when did the symptoms start?
history_cards.answer: His wife says he was completely normal until 6:45pm. He stood up from his chair at the function and his face suddenly drooped and he started speaking unclearly.
history_cards.hint_value: Sudden onset is the hallmark of stroke — not gradual like alcohol intoxication which builds over time. Time of onset determines thrombolysis eligibility

history_cards.question: Does he have atrial fibrillation or any heart condition?
history_cards.answer: Yes, AF diagnosed 6 months ago. He was given Warfarin but stopped it after 3 weeks because a small cut on his hand bled for a long time and it scared him.
history_cards.hint_value: AF without anticoagulation = very high cardioembolic stroke risk (CHADS2 score likely 3–4). This is the aetiology

history_cards.question: Is there any weakness in his arms or legs?
history_cards.answer: Yes — his wife noticed he cannot lift his right arm at all. His right leg is also weak but he can move it slightly.
history_cards.hint_value: Right-sided hemiparesis + facial droop + aphasia = left MCA territory involvement

history_cards.question: How much did he drink tonight?
history_cards.answer: Two small glasses of whisky over 3 hours. He drinks occasionally at functions but never gets this way.
history_cards.hint_value: Minimal alcohol, disproportionate neurological deficit — alcohol intoxication cannot explain focal neurological signs

history_cards.question: Any headache or vomiting?
history_cards.answer: No headache. No vomiting. His wife says he did not complain of any head pain.
history_cards.hint_value: Absence of severe headache makes haemorrhagic stroke less likely — but CT is mandatory to confirm

history_cards.question: Any similar episode before, even briefly?
history_cards.answer: Actually yes — last week he had a funny episode for about 20 minutes where his speech became slurred and then completely resolved. He said it was tiredness.
history_cards.hint_value: Previous TIA (transient ischaemic attack) is a major red flag — high risk of completed stroke within 48 hours. ABCD2 score should have been calculated then

findings.name: Blood Pressure
findings.result: 168/94 mmHg — elevated

findings.name: Pulse
findings.result: Irregular rhythm, rate 88 bpm — consistent with atrial fibrillation

findings.name: Blood Glucose (Glucometer)
findings.result: 102 mg/dL — normal. No hypoglycaemia.

findings.name: GCS
findings.result: E4V3M6 = 13/15. Alert but confused.

findings.name: Facial Nerve
findings.result: Right lower motor neurone pattern facial droop — right nasolabial fold flattened, unable to show teeth on right side.

findings.name: Power Assessment
findings.result: Right arm: 1/5. Right leg: 3/5. Left side: 5/5 normal.

imaging_options.report:
No acute haemorrhage identified.
No obvious early infarct changes — note: CT may be normal in first 6–12 hours of ischaemic stroke.
No midline shift.
No space-occupying lesion.
Old lacunar infarct in right basal ganglia.

Impression: No intracranial haemorrhage. Early ischaemic stroke cannot be excluded — clinical correlation essential. MRI diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) recommended if available.

DWI sequence: Acute restricted diffusion in left MCA territory — involving left fronto-parietal cortex and corona radiata.
ADC map: Corresponding dark signal confirming acute ischaemia.
No haemorrhagic transformation.
Old lacunar infarct right basal ganglia (T2 bright, DWI negative).

Impression: Acute left MCA territory ischaemic infarct. Findings consistent with cardioembolic aetiology given AF history.

Rhythm: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular rate, 88 bpm.
No P waves. Irregularly irregular QRS complexes.
No ST changes or LVH.

Impression: Atrial fibrillation confirmed. No acute ischaemic changes.

optimal_path.explanation: Sudden onset focal neurological deficit (facial droop, hemiparesis, aphasia) in a patient with AF off anticoagulation is a stroke until proven otherwise. First move: glucometer — rule out hypoglycaemia as a reversible mimic in 30 seconds. Then non-contrast CT head to exclude haemorrhage and determine thrombolysis eligibility. Time from onset is the most critical piece of history — window for IV tPA is 4.5 hours.

debrief.pathophysiology: Atrial fibrillation causes stagnant blood flow in the left atrial appendage, promoting thrombus formation. Warfarin discontinuation allowed the INR to normalise, removing the only anticoagulant protection. A thrombus embolised to the left middle cerebral artery, causing sudden occlusion and ischaemia of the left fronto-parietal cortex — producing contralateral (right-sided) hemiparesis, facial droop, and expressive aphasia (Broca's area involvement).
debrief.classic_presentation: Sudden onset focal neurological deficit — FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency. In AF-associated cardioembolic stroke: maximal deficit at onset (no stuttering), large vessel territory involvement, often haemorrhagic transformation on follow-up imaging.
key_discriminators: Sudden onset vs gradual (alcohol, metabolic encephalopathy)
Focal deficits — alcohol intoxication causes global confusion, not unilateral weakness
Normal glucometer — hypoglycaemia mimics stroke and must be ruled out in 30 seconds
Irregular pulse (AF) in a patient off anticoagulation = cardioembolic stroke until proven otherwise
common_mimics: Alcohol intoxication — slurred speech and unsteadiness, but bilateral, no focal motor deficits, resolves with time
Hypoglycaemia — can cause focal neurological deficits, must check glucometer immediately, corrects with dextrose
Todd's paresis — post-ictal weakness after seizure, but there is a witnessed seizure history and gradual recovery
debrief.treatment: Immediate: Check time of onset, rule out hypoglycaemia, non-contrast CT head. If ischaemic and within 4.5 hours: IV Alteplase (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg (max 90mg). If eligible for mechanical thrombectomy and large vessel occlusion confirmed on CTA: transfer to thrombectomy-capable centre immediately. Restart anticoagulation for AF: Warfarin (target INR 2–3) or a NOAC (Apixaban or Rivaroxaban) — timing of restart after stroke is debated, typically 2–4 weeks. Aspirin 150mg daily in first 2 weeks if not thrombolysed.
debrief.clinical_pearl: The 4.5-hour thrombolysis window makes time of onset the single most important piece of history in stroke. Every minute of delay = 1.9 million neurons lost. When the family says 'we don't know when exactly,' find out when he was last known to be normal — that is your onset time. In AF patients who stopped anticoagulation, ALWAYS calculate CHADS2-VASc score before discharge — it will likely be ≥ 4, meaning very high stroke risk and mandatory anticoagulation.
debrief.attendings_note:
The family told you it was alcohol and you almost wrote that in the notes. I want you to understand something fundamental: alcohol does not cause unilateral facial droop. Alcohol does not cause right arm power of 1/5 with left arm completely normal. Alcohol causes global, bilateral, symmetrical effects. Any focal neurological deficit — focal, meaning one side, one limb, one nerve — is a stroke until the CT tells you otherwise.

This patient had a TIA last week. He told his family it was tiredness. If he had come to casualty that day, we could have anticoagulated him and prevented this stroke entirely. The ABCD2 score after a TIA predicts 2-day stroke risk — his score was almost certainly 5–6, meaning very high risk. We missed him once. Do not miss him again.

And the Warfarin — he stopped it because a small cut bled. You will see this story repeatedly. The fear of bleeding from Warfarin stops patients taking it, and then they come in with a stroke instead. Counsel your AF patients: a minor cut is nothing compared to a massive stroke. If Warfarin monitoring is a burden, prescribe Apixaban — fixed dose, no monitoring needed.
debrief.india_context: Stroke mortality and disability in India are disproportionately high due to late presentation and limited thrombolysis availability outside tier-1 cities. AF-related stroke is largely preventable but anticoagulation uptake remains poor due to Warfarin monitoring burden and cost. NOACs (Apixaban, Rivaroxaban) are increasingly available but still out of reach for many patients. The FAST campaign awareness is very low in rural India.

cases.title: The Worst Headache of His Life
cases.presentation: Akash Tiwari, a 22-year-old engineering student, is brought to casualty by his hostel roommates at midnight. He has had a headache since this morning that he described as 'the worst headache of my life.' He took two Crocin tablets — no relief. By evening he developed fever and began vomiting. His roommates initially thought he had a migraine because 'he gets headaches during exams.' He is now drowsy and cannot look at the bright lights in casualty.
cases.india_context: Bacterial meningitis in India most commonly affects young adults in close-contact settings such as college hostels and army barracks. Streptococcus pneumoniae is the leading cause in adults. Diagnosis is frequently delayed because early presentation mimics viral fever or migraine. Lumbar puncture is still under-utilised in many peripheral hospitals due to unfounded fear of herniation in cases without raised ICP.

history_cards.question: How severe is the headache and how quickly did it come on?
history_cards.answer: He says it is the worst headache he has ever had — he rated it 10/10. It came on over a few hours this morning and has been getting steadily worse. Paracetamol gave no relief at all.
history_cards.hint_value: Severe headache unresponsive to analgesia with rapid progression — never dismiss in a young patient. Thunderclap headache is SAH; progressive severe headache with fever is meningitis.

history_cards.question: Any neck stiffness or difficulty bending the neck?
history_cards.answer: Yes, when his roommates tried to help him look down at his phone he said his neck hurt and he could not bend it properly.
history_cards.hint_value: Neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity) is a cardinal sign of meningism — meningeal irritation from infection or blood

history_cards.question: Any rash anywhere on the body?
history_cards.answer: One of his roommates noticed some small reddish spots on his lower legs when they were helping him to the auto. They are still there.
history_cards.hint_value: Petechial/purpuric rash in meningitis = meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise — a medical emergency. Non-blanching rash on glass test.

history_cards.question: Any similar illness in his hostel recently?
history_cards.answer: He thinks one student in another room had fever and headache last week and was admitted to hospital. He does not know the details.
history_cards.hint_value: Cluster of cases in a hostel setting raises suspicion for meningococcal disease — contact tracing and prophylaxis may be required

history_cards.question: Any ear infection, sinusitis, or dental problem recently?
history_cards.answer: He had a mild cold about 10 days ago that resolved on its own. No ear pain, no dental issues.
history_cards.hint_value: URTI preceding bacterial meningitis is a classic history — nasopharyngeal colonisation followed by haematogenous spread

history_cards.question: Any seizure or loss of consciousness?
history_cards.answer: No witnessed seizure. But his roommates say he was 'not making sense' for the last hour before they brought him in.
history_cards.hint_value: Altered sensorium in meningitis indicates severe disease — GCS monitoring and urgent LP essential

findings.name: Temperature
findings.result: 39.4°C — high fever

findings.name: Heart Rate and BP
findings.result: HR 118 bpm. BP 108/70 mmHg — slightly low, possible sepsis.

findings.name: SpO2
findings.result: 97% on room air

findings.name: GCS
findings.result: E3V4M5 = 12/15. Drowsy, oriented to name only.

findings.name: Kernig's Sign
findings.result: Positive — unable to extend knee when hip is flexed to 90°, pain and resistance noted bilaterally.

findings.name: Brudzinski's Sign
findings.result: Positive — passive neck flexion causes involuntary flexion of both knees.

findings.name: Fundoscopy
findings.result: Disc margins slightly blurred bilaterally — early papilloedema cannot be excluded.

tests.name: CSF Protein / Glucose
tests.value: Protein: 380 mg/dL (high). Glucose: 18 mg/dL. CSF:serum glucose ratio 0.19 (< 0.5 = bacterial)

imaging_options.report:
No acute intracranial haemorrhage.
No space-occupying lesion.
No significant midline shift.
Ventricles: Normal size. No hydrocephalus at this stage.
Sulci: Mildly effaced suggesting cerebral oedema.

Impression: No contraindication to lumbar puncture identified. No mass lesion or obstructive hydrocephalus. Clinical picture highly consistent with meningitis — proceed to LP.

Post-contrast T1: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement — bilateral, most prominent over convexities and basal cisterns.
DWI: No acute infarction.
No cerebral abscess.
No empyema.

Impression: Findings diagnostic of bacterial meningitis. Leptomeningeal enhancement pattern consistent with pyogenic aetiology.

Lung fields: Clear. No consolidation, no effusion.
Heart size: Normal.
No evidence of pneumonia.

Impression: Normal CXR. Pneumonia-associated bacteraemia not the source based on imaging — but does not exclude nasopharyngeal or other source.

optimal_path.explanation: Classic triad of fever + severe headache + neck stiffness in a young hostel resident should trigger immediate empirical antibiotics — do not wait for LP results. CT head first only if there is papilloedema or focal neurology (to exclude raised ICP before LP). LP is the diagnostic gold standard — turbid CSF, neutrophilic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose confirms bacterial meningitis. Blood cultures and IV Ceftriaxone must start within 30 minutes of presentation.

debrief.pathophysiology: Bacteria (most commonly S. pneumoniae in adults) colonise the nasopharynx, invade the bloodstream, and cross the blood-brain barrier into the subarachnoid space. The inflammatory response — neutrophil infiltration, cytokine release, BBB breakdown — causes cerebral oedema, raised intracranial pressure, and vasculitis. CSF glucose falls as bacteria and leukocytes consume it; protein rises from BBB leakage.
debrief.classic_presentation: Young adult with fever, severe headache (worst of life), neck stiffness, photophobia, and vomiting. Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs positive. Meningococcal disease may show petechial/purpuric rash — non-blanching on glass test.
key_discriminators: Neutrophilic pleocytosis in CSF (> 1000 cells, > 80% neutrophils) = bacterial, not viral
CSF:serum glucose ratio < 0.5 is highly specific for bacterial meningitis
Non-blanching petechial rash = meningococcaemia — do not wait for anything, give IV Benzylpenicillin immediately
Subarachnoid haemorrhage: thunderclap onset, xanthochromia in CSF, blood not white cells
common_mimics: Viral meningitis — less severe, CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (not neutrophilic), glucose normal, protein mildly elevated, self-limiting
Subarachnoid haemorrhage — thunderclap headache, CSF shows xanthochromia and RBCs, CT often diagnostic
Severe migraine — recurrent history, no fever, no meningism, responds to triptans, normal CSF
debrief.treatment: Do not delay antibiotics for imaging. If any delay in LP: start IV Ceftriaxone 2g every 12 hours + Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg every 6 hours (reduces neurological complications) immediately. After blood cultures, proceed to CT head. If CT clear, do LP. Add Ampicillin if Listeria suspected (immunocompromised, elderly). Monitor ICP, treat cerebral oedema with head elevation and Mannitol if needed. Rifampicin prophylaxis for close contacts in meningococcal disease.
debrief.clinical_pearl: In suspected bacterial meningitis, do not delay antibiotics to get a CT or LP. Every hour of delay in antibiotic administration increases mortality by 30%. Give IV Ceftriaxone first, then investigate. If non-blanching rash is present, give IV Benzylpenicillin in casualty immediately — this is one of the few true 'seconds count' scenarios in medicine.
debrief.attendings_note:
The roommates said migraine and you almost wrote that diagnosis. But this man has never had a headache this severe, he has fever, and he cannot tolerate light. Migraine does not cause fever. Migraine does not cause neck stiffness. When I see a young person with the triad of fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness, I am giving antibiotics before I even order the CT — because if I am wrong I have given one dose of Ceftriaxone unnecessarily, but if I am right and I wait, I may be signing his death certificate.

Look at those spots on his legs. Put a glass on them. Do they blanch? They do not. That is a non-blanching petechial rash — meningococcaemia. That is the most time-critical diagnosis in medicine. If you see that rash, give IV Benzylpenicillin before the patient leaves triage. Do not wait for results. Do not wait for the consultant. Give it.

For the LP — students are always afraid to do it, afraid of coning. Check the fundus, check for focal neurology, get the CT if there is any doubt. But once it is clear — do the LP. That CSF result is how we choose the right antibiotic and how we confirm the diagnosis. A clear LP indication should not wait until morning.
debrief.india_context: S. pneumoniae is the leading cause of bacterial meningitis in Indian adults. Meningococcal meningitis occurs in clusters, especially in crowded hostel and barracks settings. Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV) is now part of the national immunisation schedule but coverage in the 22-year-old cohort is zero — this generation was not vaccinated. Chemoprophylaxis with Rifampicin or Ciprofloxacin for close contacts is recommended in meningococcal disease.

cases.title: Three Courses of Antibiotics

patient.name: Suresh Mistry
patient.occupation: Daily wage construction labourer, migrant from Jharkhand, lives in Mumbai chawl

cases.presentation: Suresh Mistry, 28 years old, comes to the government hospital OPD. He has had a cough for 3 months that has not resolved despite being given three different courses of antibiotics by local practitioners — Amoxicillin, Azithromycin, and Co-trimoxazole in sequence. For the past 3 weeks the cough has been bringing up blood-streaked sputum. He has lost about 8kg over the last 2 months and his friends say he looks much thinner than before. He has been ignoring the illness because 'work cannot stop.'
cases.india_context: India has the highest TB burden globally — 27% of all new TB cases worldwide. Migrants from high-prevalence states (Jharkhand, Bihar, UP, Chhattisgarh) living in dense urban settings like Mumbai chawls are at very high risk. Delayed diagnosis due to repeated antibiotic courses from private practitioners is one of the most common patterns seen in government hospitals. All TB cases must be notified to NIKSHAY portal under the National TB Elimination Programme (NTEP).

history_cards.question: How long has the cough been going on, and has he had blood in the sputum?
history_cards.answer: Cough for 3 months. Blood-streaked sputum for the last 3 weeks. Not large amounts, but every morning there is some blood mixed with mucus.
history_cards.hint_value: Cough > 2 weeks + haemoptysis = TB screening mandatory in India. This is the WHO/NTEP definition of presumptive TB.

history_cards.question: Any significant weight loss or loss of appetite?
history_cards.answer: Yes, he has lost 8kg in about 2 months. He says he has no appetite and feels weak all the time. His trousers are very loose now.
history_cards.hint_value: Constitutional symptoms — weight loss, anorexia, fatigue — are hallmarks of TB. Significant weight loss in a young man must be explained.

history_cards.question: Any fever, especially in the evenings, and any night sweats?
history_cards.answer: He says he feels warm in the evenings but has not checked his temperature. His wife says his shirt is often wet when he wakes up — she washes it every day.
history_cards.hint_value: Evening low-grade fever + drenching night sweats = classic constitutional TB symptom triad with weight loss (B symptoms)

history_cards.question: Has anyone at home or at work had TB or a similar illness?
history_cards.answer: His chacha (uncle) in Jharkhand was treated for TB about 2 years ago. He lived with him for 6 months before coming to Mumbai.
history_cards.hint_value: Close contact with a TB case is a major risk factor — household exposure to smear-positive TB has 15–20% transmission risk

history_cards.question: Has he ever been tested or treated for TB before?
history_cards.answer: No, never. He says the local doctor just kept giving him different antibiotics and never sent him for any test.
history_cards.hint_value: No prior TB treatment = new case. Important for NTEP categorisation and treatment regimen choice

history_cards.question: Any alcohol use or other illnesses like diabetes?
history_cards.answer: He drinks occasionally on weekends. No diabetes that he knows of. He has never had a blood test.
history_cards.hint_value: Alcohol and diabetes are risk factors for TB and treatment complications. Worth noting for baseline assessment.

findings.name: Temperature
findings.result: 37.9°C — low-grade fever

findings.name: Weight and BMI
findings.result: 52kg, height 168cm. BMI 18.4 — underweight. Visibly cachectic.

findings.name: SpO2 and RR
findings.result: SpO2 94% on room air. RR 22/min — mildly elevated.

findings.name: Percussion
findings.result: Dullness on percussion over right upper zone and right infraclavicular area.

findings.name: Auscultation
findings.result: Bronchial breathing in right upper zone. Post-tussive crepitations in right upper and middle zone. Amphoric breath sounds (cavity).

tests.name: CBNAAT / GeneXpert (Cartridge-Based NAAT)
tests.value: M. tuberculosis DETECTED. Rifampicin resistance: NOT DETECTED.

imaging_options.report:
Right upper lobe: Large heterogeneous opacity with central lucency — thick-walled cavity approximately 4cm diameter. Surrounding consolidation and satellite nodules. Fibrotic streaking toward hilum.
Left upper lobe: Small nodular opacity — possible contralateral seeding.
No pleural effusion.
No pneumothorax.

Impression: Bilateral upper lobe disease with cavitation. Appearances highly consistent with active pulmonary tuberculosis. Recommend sputum AFB and CBNAAT.

Right upper lobe: Thick-walled cavity (4.2 × 3.8 cm) with surrounding tree-in-bud pattern and centrilobular nodules extending into right middle lobe — active endobronchial spread.
Left upper lobe: Centrilobular nodules and ground-glass opacity — early disease.
Mediastinal lymphadenopathy: Right paratracheal and subcarinal nodes enlarged (largest 1.8cm), with low-density centre suggesting necrosis.
No pleural empyema.

Impression: Active pulmonary tuberculosis with cavitation and endobronchial spread bilaterally. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy with central necrosis.

No calcified mesenteric or para-aortic lymph nodes identified.
No free air under diaphragm.
Bowel gas pattern unremarkable.

Impression: No abdominal features of TB on plain film. CT abdomen would be required if extrapulmonary involvement suspected.

optimal_path.explanation: Cough > 2 weeks + haemoptysis + weight loss + night sweats + TB contact history in a migrant labourer = presumptive TB until proven otherwise. CXR is the first investigation — upper lobe cavitation is highly characteristic. Sputum AFB smear and CBNAAT confirms the diagnosis and rules out rifampicin resistance simultaneously. HIV test is mandatory in all TB cases. Do not delay starting NTEP treatment waiting for culture results.

debrief.pathophysiology: Post-primary (reactivation) TB occurs when latent Mycobacterium tuberculosis reactivates in the upper lobes due to waning immunity, malnutrition, or new exposure. High oxygen tension in the upper lobes favours mycobacterial growth. Caseous necrosis liquefies and drains into a bronchus, forming a cavity. Cavitary TB is highly infectious — sputum contains millions of bacilli per mL. Haematogenous and endobronchial spread can seed other lung zones and distant organs.
debrief.classic_presentation: Young adult from a high-prevalence area with cough > 2 weeks, haemoptysis, low-grade evening fever, drenching night sweats, and significant weight loss. Upper zone dullness and bronchial breathing. CXR shows upper lobe consolidation with cavitation.
key_discriminators: Duration > 2 weeks + upper lobe involvement — pneumonia resolves faster, involves lower lobes more
Cavitation with surrounding consolidation and tree-in-bud pattern = active TB until proven otherwise
AFB smear and GeneXpert — direct confirmation, also tests for rifampicin resistance
TB contact history + migrant from high-prevalence state + overcrowded living = very high pre-test probability
common_mimics: Community-acquired pneumonia — acute onset, responds to antibiotics, lower lobe, no weight loss
Lung abscess — foul-smelling sputum, putrid odour, often secondary to aspiration, lower lobe, anaerobes
Lung carcinoma with cavitation — older patient, smoker, irregular thick-walled cavity, hilar mass, no fever
debrief.treatment: Notify immediately on NIKSHAY portal (mandatory under NTEP). Initiate Category 1 DOTS (Directly Observed Treatment Short course): 2HRZE/4HR. Intensive phase 2 months: Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R) + Pyrazinamide (Z) + Ethambutol (E) daily. Continuation phase 4 months: Isoniazid + Rifampicin daily. Pyridoxine (B6) with Isoniazid to prevent neuropathy. Baseline LFTs, uric acid, visual acuity. Nutritional support and Nikshay Poshan Yojana (Rs 500/month food support).
debrief.clinical_pearl: In India, any patient with cough > 2 weeks must be screened for TB — this is the NTEP definition of presumptive TB. GeneXpert (CBNAAT) is now available free at all government hospitals and simultaneously diagnoses TB and Rifampicin resistance in 2 hours. Never start empirical anti-TB treatment without microbiological confirmation — drug-resistant TB is a growing crisis and incorrect treatment creates resistance.
debrief.attendings_note:
Three courses of antibiotics over 3 months — and nobody thought to check for TB. This is the most common pattern I see, and it bothers me every time. In India, cough for more than 2 weeks is TB until the test proves otherwise. It is not 'recurrent chest infection.' It is not 'antibiotic-resistant bacteria.' It is TB until you have a negative sputum result. Every day of delay is another few hundred people this man has exposed in his chawl and at his construction site.

Cavitary TB is the most infectious form. When you see a cavity in the upper lobe of a young person from Bihar or Jharkhand or Odisha who has been coughing for months, you should be moving fast. GeneXpert is free at government hospitals and gives you the answer in 2 hours — use it. And test for HIV in every single TB patient without exception. HIV-TB coinfection changes everything about management.

NIKSHAY notification is not optional — it is the law. And DOTS compliance matters. A patient who takes irregular treatment is more dangerous than one who never treated at all, because he will develop resistance. Make sure someone is watching him take those tablets.
debrief.india_context: India contributes 27% of global TB incidence. The National TB Elimination Programme (NTEP) targets TB elimination by 2025. Key features: free diagnosis (sputum AFB, CBNAAT, culture), free treatment (DOTS), Nikshay Poshan Yojana (nutritional support), mandatory NIKSHAY notification. Drug-resistant TB (MDR-TB: resistant to H and R; XDR-TB: additionally resistant to fluoroquinolones and injectables) is a growing challenge — GeneXpert detects Rifampicin resistance as a proxy for MDR.

cases.title: Ten Days After Joy

patient.name: Priya Mehta
patient.occupation: Homemaker, 10 days post normal vaginal delivery

cases.presentation: Priya Mehta, 29 years old, is brought to casualty by her husband 10 days after delivering her first baby by normal vaginal delivery at a nursing home. Since yesterday she has been increasingly breathless and today she had sudden worsening with mild right-sided chest pain on breathing deeply. Her husband says she has been very anxious since the delivery and 'not sleeping well' — he thought this breathlessness was post-delivery anxiety or weakness. She has not had a fever. On examination she looks frightened, is tachypnoeic, and is holding her chest.
cases.india_context: Pulmonary embolism is significantly underdiagnosed in India — post-partum PE is a leading cause of maternal mortality that is frequently misattributed to post-partum anaemia, cardiac disease, or anxiety. Virchow's triad (hypercoagulability, venous stasis, endothelial damage) is maximally activated in the post-partum period. D-dimer has limited utility post-partum as it is physiologically elevated, making clinical scoring (Wells score) and CTPA essential.

history_cards.question: Did she have any swelling or pain in one leg after delivery?
history_cards.answer: Yes — her husband mentions that her left calf has been swollen and tender for the past 3 days. They thought it was because she was lying down too much after delivery.
history_cards.hint_value: Left calf swelling and tenderness = DVT until proven otherwise. DVT + breathlessness = PE until proven otherwise. This is Virchow's triad in action.

history_cards.question: How exactly does the chest pain feel? Does it change with breathing?
history_cards.answer: Sharp, stabbing pain on the right side. Worse when she takes a deep breath or coughs. Better when she breathes shallowly.
history_cards.hint_value: Pleuritic chest pain (worse on inspiration) = pleural irritation — seen in PE (pulmonary infarction) and pneumonia. In post-partum context with DVT, PE is the diagnosis.

history_cards.question: Has she been moving around and active since delivery, or mostly in bed?
history_cards.answer: She had a difficult labour — 18 hours — and has been mostly resting in bed since. She barely gets up even to feed the baby.
history_cards.hint_value: Prolonged immobility after delivery = venous stasis. Combined with post-partum hypercoagulability = DVT/PE risk maximised.

history_cards.question: Any previous clot in the leg or lung, or family history of clotting problems?
history_cards.answer: No previous clots. But her mother had 'some problem with blood clotting' — she doesn't remember the details.
history_cards.hint_value: Family history of thrombophilia raises suspicion — inherited thrombophilias (Factor V Leiden, Protein C/S deficiency) increase VTE risk

history_cards.question: Any fever or cough with coloured sputum?
history_cards.answer: No fever at all. Dry cough only, no coloured sputum.
history_cards.hint_value: Absence of fever and productive cough makes pneumonia less likely — PE is typically afebrile or low-grade fever only

history_cards.question: Was she on any blood thinners or did anyone warn her about clot risk after delivery?
history_cards.answer: No one mentioned anything about blood clots. She was discharged from the nursing home the next day after delivery.
history_cards.hint_value: Early discharge without VTE prophylaxis after prolonged labour is a missed opportunity — a systems issue worth noting

findings.name: Heart Rate and BP
findings.result: HR 118 bpm — tachycardic. BP 98/66 mmHg — borderline hypotensive.

findings.name: SpO2 and RR
findings.result: SpO2 89% on room air — critically low. RR 28/min — tachypnoeic.

findings.name: Temperature
findings.result: 37.4°C — afebrile

findings.name: Left Calf
findings.result: Left calf circumference 3cm greater than right. Tenderness on palpation of left calf. Homan's sign positive.

findings.name: Chest Auscultation
findings.result: Decreased breath sounds right base. Pleural rub audible right lower zone. No wheeze.

tests.name: D-Dimer
tests.normal_range: < 500 ng/mL (note: physiologically elevated post-partum — not useful for exclusion)

tests.name: ABG
tests.value: pH 7.48, PaO2 54 mmHg, PaCO2 28 mmHg, HCO3 21 — type 1 respiratory failure with respiratory alkalosis

imaging_options.report:
CTPA (CT Pulmonary Angiography):
Filling defect in right main pulmonary artery extending into right lower lobe segmental and subsegmental arteries. Filling defect in left lower lobe segmental artery.
Right lower lobe: Wedge-shaped peripheral opacity abutting pleura — pulmonary infarction.
Right heart: Mildly dilated RV with septal flattening ('D-sign') — RV strain.
No pleural empyema. Small right pleural effusion.

Impression: Bilateral pulmonary embolism with right heart strain. Pulmonary infarction right lower lobe.

Rate: 118 bpm. Rhythm: Sinus tachycardia.
S1Q3T3 pattern: S wave in lead I, Q wave and T-wave inversion in lead III.
New right bundle branch block (RBBB).
T-wave inversions V1–V4.

Impression: ECG changes consistent with acute right heart strain from pulmonary embolism. S1Q3T3 is present but not specific — sinus tachycardia alone is the most common ECG finding in PE.

Left popliteal and femoral vein: Non-compressible. Echogenic thrombus within lumen. No flow on colour Doppler.
Right leg veins: Compressible, normal flow.

Impression: Left femoro-popliteal DVT confirmed. Findings consistent with DVT as the source of pulmonary embolism.

optimal_path.explanation: Post-partum breathlessness + pleuritic chest pain + unilateral calf swelling = PE until proven otherwise. Wells score likely ≥ 6 (high probability). In high clinical probability, go straight to CTPA — do not wait for D-dimer, which is useless post-partum as it is always elevated. ABG showing type 1 respiratory failure + respiratory alkalosis supports diagnosis. Troponin and BNP elevation indicates RV strain — risk stratification matters for treatment intensity. Start anticoagulation empirically if CTPA is delayed.

debrief.pathophysiology: Pregnancy and the post-partum period cause all three components of Virchow's triad: hypercoagulability (increased clotting factors, decreased protein S), venous stasis (IVC compression by uterus, immobility), and endothelial injury (delivery trauma). DVT forms in deep leg veins and a fragment embolises to pulmonary vasculature. Obstruction of pulmonary arteries increases right ventricular afterload, causing RV strain, reduced LV preload, and potentially cardiovascular collapse in massive PE.
debrief.classic_presentation: Post-partum woman with sudden pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, tachycardia, hypoxia, and unilateral leg swelling. In sub-massive PE: tachycardia, hypoxia, RV strain on ECG/Echo. In massive PE: haemodynamic instability, syncope, cardiac arrest.
key_discriminators: Post-partum period + immobility + leg swelling = DVT/PE triad, not anxiety
Pleuritic chest pain (worse on inspiration) + hypoxia + normal CXR = classic PE pattern
D-dimer is NOT useful post-partum — always elevated — use Wells score and proceed to CTPA
S1Q3T3 + sinus tachycardia + RBBB on ECG = right heart strain from PE
common_mimics: Post-partum cardiomyopathy — biventricular failure, cardiomegaly on CXR, low EF on Echo, bilateral oedema
Post-partum anxiety/panic — tachycardia and breathlessness, but SpO2 normal, no leg swelling, ABG shows respiratory alkalosis only
Pneumonia — fever, productive cough, focal consolidation on CXR, responds to antibiotics
debrief.treatment: Start anticoagulation immediately if clinical suspicion is high and no absolute contraindications. Low Molecular Weight Heparin (LMWH — Enoxaparin) is safe while breastfeeding. For haemodynamically stable PE: LMWH therapeutic dose, transition to Warfarin or DOAC at 6 weeks post-partum. For massive PE with haemodynamic compromise: systemic thrombolysis (Alteplase) if no surgical contraindication. Duration of anticoagulation: minimum 3–6 months, reassess thrombophilia workup at 3 months post-event.
debrief.clinical_pearl: D-dimer is physiologically elevated in pregnancy and the post-partum period — a 'positive' D-dimer tells you nothing and a 'negative' D-dimer is very unusual. Never use D-dimer to rule out PE in post-partum patients. Use the modified Wells score: if ≥ 5 (high probability), go straight to CTPA. Post-partum PE is a leading cause of maternal mortality in India and it is almost entirely preventable with VTE risk assessment and LMWH prophylaxis at discharge.
debrief.attendings_note:
The husband said 'anxiety after delivery' and you nearly wrote that in the notes. I want you to remember this face forever — a 29-year-old mother, 10 days post-partum, with a baby at home. She nearly died because someone thought she was just anxious. Post-partum breathlessness is PE until proven otherwise. That is the rule. No exceptions.

Look at her leg. Left calf 3cm bigger than the right. That is not post-partum oedema — post-partum oedema is bilateral. That is a DVT. DVT plus sudden breathlessness in a post-partum woman is a pulmonary embolism. The diagnosis was there in the history and examination — we did not need any test to make the decision to anticoagulate.

And the D-dimer — I see junior doctors ordering D-dimer in post-partum women and then panicking when it comes back at 3000. Of course it is 3000. It is always elevated post-partum. It is useless. Use the Wells score. If it is high probability, go straight to CTPA. Start LMWH while you are waiting for the CT. Do not wait.
debrief.india_context: Post-partum VTE is a major contributor to maternal mortality in India. Many nursing homes discharge patients within 24–48 hours without VTE risk assessment or LMWH prophylaxis. The WHO safe childbirth checklist includes VTE risk assessment, but compliance is patchy. LMWH (Enoxaparin, Clexane) is available in India but not routinely prescribed at discharge from low-resource settings. Thrombophilia screening (Protein C, S, Antithrombin III, Factor V Leiden) should be done at 3 months post-event.

cases.title: The Wrong Suspect
cases.presentation: Harpreet Kaur, 45 years old, is brought to casualty with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back that started 6 hours ago after a heavy meal at a family wedding. The pain is constant, 9/10 in severity, and has not improved with antacids or Buscopan. She has vomited 4 times. She is overweight and the triage nurse has noted in the file: 'known occasional drinker — alcohol-related?' The patient herself insists she only had 2 small drinks at the wedding.
cases.india_context: Gallstone disease is the leading cause of acute pancreatitis in Indian women, particularly those who are obese, multiparous, and over 40 (the 4 F's: Fat, Female, Fertile, Forty). Alcohol is the dominant cause in men. The triage assumption of alcohol in a woman with pancreatitis is a common and harmful cognitive bias seen in Indian hospitals.

history_cards.question: Has she had similar episodes of pain before, especially after fatty meals?
history_cards.answer: Yes, actually — she has had three or four episodes over the past year of pain in the upper abdomen and right side after eating heavy food. They lasted a few hours and went away on their own. She never investigated them.
history_cards.hint_value: Recurrent right upper quadrant / epigastric pain after fatty meals = biliary colic from gallstones. This is the first attack severe enough to present to hospital.

history_cards.question: Exactly how much alcohol has she had in the last 48 hours?
history_cards.answer: Two small glasses of wine at the wedding yesterday — that is all. She drinks maybe once or twice a month at most. No bingeing, no daily drinking.
history_cards.hint_value: Minimal alcohol intake makes alcoholic pancreatitis very unlikely — need to redirect investigation to gallstones as the cause

history_cards.question: Has she had any jaundice or yellow eyes recently?
history_cards.answer: Actually, yes — her husband noticed her eyes were slightly yellow about a week ago, for a day or two. It resolved on its own.
history_cards.hint_value: Transient jaundice followed by pancreatitis = gallstone passed through CBD, causing transient obstruction. Classic pattern of gallstone pancreatitis.

history_cards.question: Any fever or chills?
history_cards.answer: Mild fever — 38.1°C at triage. No rigors or chills.
history_cards.hint_value: Mild fever is common in acute pancreatitis from inflammation. High fever + rigors would suggest ascending cholangitis (Charcot's triad).

history_cards.question: Any history of diabetes, high triglycerides, or calcium problems?
history_cards.answer: No diabetes. Not sure about triglycerides — never checked. No family history of calcium problems.
history_cards.hint_value: Hypertriglyceridaemia and hypercalcaemia are other causes of pancreatitis — important to exclude if gallstones not found on USG

history_cards.question: Any medications — steroids, diuretics, or new medicines recently?
history_cards.answer: She takes Metformin for pre-diabetes. No steroids. No new medicines.
history_cards.hint_value: Drug-induced pancreatitis is rare but Metformin is not a known cause — low yield here but good practice to check medication history

findings.name: Temperature and HR
findings.result: Temp 38.2°C. HR 108 bpm — tachycardic.

findings.name: Blood Pressure
findings.result: 104/68 mmHg — borderline low, likely hypovolaemia from vomiting.

findings.name: BMI
findings.result: BMI 31.4 — obese. Centripetal adiposity noted.

findings.name: Tenderness
findings.result: Severe epigastric and right hypochondrial tenderness. Guarding present in epigastrium. No rebound tenderness. Murphy's sign borderline positive.

findings.name: Flanks and Umbilicus
findings.result: No Grey Turner sign (flank bruising). No Cullen sign (periumbilical bruising). Bowel sounds reduced.

imaging_options.report:
Gallbladder: Multiple echogenic foci with posterior shadowing — cholelithiasis (multiple stones, largest 1.4cm). Gallbladder wall mildly thickened (4mm). Pericholecystic fluid — mild cholecystitis.
Common Bile Duct: 9mm (mildly dilated — normal < 6mm). No obvious CBD stone identified on USG (sensitivity for CBD stones only 30–50%).
Pancreas: Poorly visualised due to overlying bowel gas. Peripancreatic fat stranding noted where visible.
No free fluid in abdomen.

Impression: Cholelithiasis with mild cholecystitis. CBD mildly dilated suggesting recent or ongoing stone passage. Pancreas poorly assessed — CECT abdomen recommended for complete pancreatitis evaluation.

CECT Abdomen (Contrast-Enhanced CT):
Pancreas: Diffuse oedematous enlargement. Peripancreatic fat stranding. Peripancreatic fluid collection around head and body. No pancreatic necrosis (> 30% enhancement preserved).
Modified CT Severity Index (CTSI): Pancreatitis grade C + peripancreatic fluid = score 4 (moderate severity).
Gallbladder: Multiple gallstones. Mildly thickened wall. Small pericholecystic fluid.
CBD: 9mm, no stone visualised.
No free intraperitoneal air. No abscess.

Impression: Moderate acute pancreatitis (CTSI 4). No necrosis. Consistent with gallstone aetiology.

Sentinel loop sign: Localised ileus in epigastric region — mildly dilated jejunal loop adjacent to pancreatic region.
No free gas under diaphragm.
No radio-opaque gallstones (only 10–15% of gallstones are radio-opaque).
No obvious calcification in pancreatic region.

Impression: Sentinel loop — supportive of pancreatic inflammation. Limited value in pancreatitis workup.

optimal_path.explanation: Severe epigastric pain radiating to back + vomiting in an obese middle-aged woman after a fatty meal = gallstone pancreatitis until proven otherwise. Serum lipase > 3x upper limit confirms pancreatitis. Elevated ALT and bilirubin point toward biliary aetiology — ALT > 150 U/L has 85% PPV for gallstone pancreatitis. USG confirms gallstones. CECT abdomen (Balthazar scoring) needed if severe disease suspected. Do not assume alcohol without a robust history.

debrief.pathophysiology: Gallstones passing through or impacting at the ampulla of Vater obstruct the pancreatic duct, causing backpressure and premature activation of pancreatic enzymes within the gland itself. Trypsinogen auto-activates to trypsin, triggering a cascade of enzyme activation causing acinar cell auto-digestion, inflammation, and oedema. In severe cases: necrosis, haemorrhage, and systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS).
debrief.classic_presentation: Obese middle-aged woman with sudden severe epigastric pain radiating straight through to the back, precipitated by a fatty meal. Vomiting prominent. Epigastric guarding. Serum lipase > 3× normal. Gallstones on USG.
key_discriminators: ALT > 150 U/L in pancreatitis = gallstone aetiology with 85% PPV — alcohol pancreatitis rarely raises ALT this much
Lipase more specific than amylase for pancreatitis — amylase can be elevated in many conditions
Transient jaundice preceding attack = classic gallstone pancreatitis pattern (CBD stone passed)
4 F's: Fat, Female, Forty, Fertile — classic demographic for gallstone disease
common_mimics: Peptic ulcer perforation — sudden severe pain, board-like rigidity, free air under diaphragm on erect CXR, amylase mildly elevated
Acute cholecystitis — RUQ pain, Murphy's sign positive, fever, but amylase not dramatically elevated
Mesenteric ischaemia — pain out of proportion to examination, older patient, AF or vascular disease, lactic acidosis
debrief.treatment: Aggressive IV fluids (Ringer's Lactate preferred over normal saline — reduces acidosis risk): 250–500 mL/hr initially. Nil by mouth. Nasogastric tube if vomiting uncontrolled. IV analgesia — Tramadol or Morphine (morphine does not worsen pancreatitis despite old myths). Antibiotics only if infected necrosis suspected (not routine). ERCP + sphincterotomy if CBD stone confirmed or cholangitis suspected. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy before discharge (same admission if mild, within 6 weeks if moderate/severe) to prevent recurrence. Ranson/BISAP criteria for severity stratification.
debrief.clinical_pearl: Serum lipase is more sensitive and specific for pancreatitis than amylase — many labs now only report lipase. An ALT > 150 U/L in acute pancreatitis should immediately shift your thinking to gallstones as the cause, not alcohol. This single finding has an 85% positive predictive value for gallstone pancreatitis. Do not miss this — it changes both the cause you document and whether ERCP is needed.
debrief.attendings_note:
The triage nurse wrote 'known occasional drinker' and suddenly everyone was thinking alcohol pancreatitis. This is a bias that harms patients — particularly women. The vast majority of pancreatitis in Indian women is from gallstones, not alcohol. And this patient told you clearly — two glasses of wine. That cannot cause pancreatitis.

Look at the real story: she is 45, female, overweight, had three or four previous episodes of pain after fatty food that went away on their own. Those were biliary colic attacks — gallstones causing transient duct obstruction. And she had yellow eyes last week for a day. That was a stone passing through her bile duct, causing transient jaundice. Classic gallstone pancreatitis story. The alcohol note in the file almost derailed the whole workup.

Remember the ALT. If the ALT is above 150 in pancreatitis, you should be thinking gallstones. Alcohol pancreatitis raises ALT a little — not to 188. That number alone should have told you the cause before the ultrasound. Use your labs intelligently.
debrief.india_context: Gallstone disease in India predominantly affects the obese, multiparous woman over 40. Cholesterol gallstones are commoner in urban populations with high-fat diets; pigment stones (secondary to haemolytic anaemias like sickle cell, thalassaemia) are seen more in rural areas. The misconception that pancreatitis is always alcohol-related leads to gender bias in triage and missed biliary diagnoses. Early cholecystectomy in the same admission reduces readmission rates significantly.

related_cases.teaser: 50M alcoholic, CECT shows 60% pancreatic necrosis, SIRS criteria met, ICU transfer
related_cases.teaches: Severe necrotising pancreatitis — Balthazar grading, BISAP score, ICU criteria, infected necrosis

related_cases.label: Classic mimic
related_cases.teaser: 60M sudden onset severe epigastric pain, board-like abdomen, erect CXR shows free air
related_cases.teaches: Perforated peptic ulcer — the surgical emergency that looks like pancreatitis but kills faster

related_cases.label: Same chief complaint, different diagnosis
related_cases.teaser: 55F, RUQ pain after fatty food, fever 39°C, Murphy's sign strongly positive, no epigastric radiation
related_cases.teaches: Acute cholecystitis vs gallstone pancreatitis — how to distinguish clinically and which needs surgery urgently

cases.title: Yellow Eyes After Vacation

patient.name: Karthik Subramaniam
patient.occupation: First-year engineering college student, Tamil Nadu, recently returned from village home

cases.presentation: Karthik Subramaniam, 19 years old, is brought to college health centre by his hostel warden. He returned from his village home in Tirunelveli 10 days ago and has been unwell since. He has had low-grade fever, nausea, and complete loss of appetite for a week, and since yesterday his eyes have turned yellow. He is exhausted and barely eating. His roommates are worried because two other students who went home during the same break also came back unwell.
cases.india_context: Hepatitis A is endemic in India and outbreaks are common in areas with poor sanitation and contaminated water supply. The classic Indian pattern is post-monsoon outbreaks in villages and urban slums with flooding. Young adults from semi-urban or rural areas who have not developed natural immunity are at particular risk. Cluster cases in a hostel after a break are a hallmark pattern of HAV.

history_cards.question: What did he eat and drink during his village visit?
history_cards.answer: He ate home food, street food from the local market, and drank water from the overhead tank. He also had fresh sugarcane juice from a street stall and ate cut fruit from a vendor.
history_cards.hint_value: Contaminated water and raw food from street vendors are the classic transmission routes for HAV. Sugarcane juice and cut fruit are high-risk vehicles.

history_cards.question: Did anyone else at home or in the village fall ill around the same time?
history_cards.answer: Yes — two younger cousins in the village also had fever and jaundice. His mother said there were several cases of jaundice in the village last month after the rains.
history_cards.hint_value: Cluster of jaundice cases in a community after heavy rains and flooding = HAV outbreak pattern. Epidemiological link strongly supports diagnosis.

history_cards.question: Describe the jaundice — when exactly did it start and is the urine dark?
history_cards.answer: Eyes turned yellow yesterday. Urine has been dark — tea-coloured — for 3 days. Stools have become pale/clay-coloured.
history_cards.hint_value: Dark urine (bilirubinuria) preceding jaundice + pale stools = obstructive jaundice pattern — but in HAV this is hepatocellular with intrahepatic cholestasis

history_cards.question: Any abdominal pain?
history_cards.answer: Yes — dull ache in the upper right side of his abdomen. Not severe, but uncomfortable, especially when he presses.
history_cards.hint_value: Right hypochondrial discomfort = hepatomegaly with capsular stretch — liver is enlarged and tender

history_cards.question: Has he had jaundice before, or any liver problem?
history_cards.answer: No, never. This is the first time. He has never had any liver tests done.
history_cards.hint_value: First episode in a teenager — HAV is the classic first viral hepatitis in young Indians who lack prior exposure

history_cards.question: Any injections, blood transfusions, tattoos, or sexual activity that could explain hepatitis?
history_cards.answer: No injections, no tattoos, no transfusions. He is not sexually active.
history_cards.hint_value: Screens for parenterally transmitted hepatitis (B and C) — negative history makes HAV or HEV more likely, consistent with feco-oral route

findings.name: Temperature
findings.result: 37.7°C — low-grade fever. Pulse 88 bpm. BP 118/74 mmHg.

findings.name: General Appearance
findings.result: Icteric sclera bilaterally. Mild jaundice of skin. No pallor. No clubbing.

findings.name: Liver
findings.result: Hepatomegaly — liver 3cm below right costal margin, tender on palpation. Smooth surface, no nodularity.

findings.name: Spleen and Ascites
findings.result: Spleen just palpable at left costal margin — mild splenomegaly. No ascites. No caput medusae.

imaging_options.report:
Liver: Mildly enlarged (span 16cm). Diffusely heterogeneous echotexture with periportal oedema — 'starry sky' appearance. No focal lesion. No biliary dilatation.
Gallbladder: Wall mildly thickened (4mm). No gallstones.
Spleen: Mildly enlarged (13cm).
Portal vein: Normal calibre and flow.
No ascites.

Impression: Hepatomegaly with periportal oedema and 'starry sky' pattern — consistent with acute viral hepatitis. No biliary obstruction (rules out obstructive jaundice).

No free air under diaphragm.
Soft tissue shadow in RUQ consistent with hepatomegaly.
No calcification.

Impression: Limited utility in viral hepatitis — included only to exclude other abdominal pathology.

Liver: Returned to normal size (span 14cm). Normal echotexture. Periportal oedema resolved.
Spleen: Normal.
No ascites, no biliary dilatation.

Impression: Complete sonographic resolution consistent with self-limiting acute hepatitis A. No chronic liver disease changes.

optimal_path.explanation: Young person, post-travel, cluster of cases, contaminated food/water history — the pre-test probability for HAV is very high. LFTs confirm hepatocellular pattern (very high ALT/AST, elevated bilirubin). Anti-HAV IgM is the confirmatory test — positive = acute infection. INR is crucial to check — if prolonged, it signals hepatic failure risk. Ultrasound confirms hepatomegaly and rules out obstructive jaundice. HBsAg and anti-HCV rule out other viral hepatitis.

debrief.pathophysiology: HAV is a non-enveloped RNA virus transmitted via the feco-oral route — contaminated food and water. It replicates in hepatocytes, causing immune-mediated cell damage rather than direct cytopathic effect. ALT/AST elevation reflects hepatocyte destruction. Conjugated bilirubin cannot be excreted normally — spills into urine (dark urine) and the gut receives less bile (pale stools). HAV almost never causes chronic liver disease — 99.9% recover fully.
debrief.classic_presentation: Young person from or returning from endemic area, low-grade fever and malaise for 1–2 weeks (prodromal phase), then jaundice with dark urine and pale stools, tender hepatomegaly. Cluster of similar cases in family or community. Self-limiting, recovers in 4–6 weeks.
key_discriminators: Feco-oral transmission history (contaminated food/water) + cluster of cases = HAV or HEV
Anti-HAV IgM positive = current acute infection (Anti-HAV IgG = past infection/immunity)
ALT >> ALP ratio = hepatocellular pattern, not obstructive — USG shows no bile duct dilatation
Young patient, no parenteral risk factors, no sexual history = HAV most likely, not HBV/HCV
common_mimics: Hepatitis E — identical clinical picture, also feco-oral, also epidemic after flooding; differentiated by anti-HEV IgM; dangerous in pregnancy
Leptospirosis — also post-flooding, fever + jaundice + renal failure + conjunctival suffusion; Weil's disease; check anti-Leptospira MAT
Drug-induced liver injury — medication history essential; no epidemic pattern; no specific serology
debrief.treatment: Supportive — no specific antiviral. Rest, oral hydration, high-carbohydrate low-fat diet. Avoid alcohol completely for 6 months. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs (paracetamol, NSAIDs, statins). No isolation needed if standard hygiene maintained. Monitor INR — if rising, indicates acute liver failure risk and needs hospital admission. Discharge if eating and drinking adequately, INR < 1.5, no encephalopathy. Notify to health authorities for outbreak investigation. HAV vaccine for close contacts.
debrief.clinical_pearl: INR is the most important monitoring parameter in acute viral hepatitis — it reflects hepatic synthetic function. A normal INR (as here) means the liver, though inflamed, is still synthesising clotting factors. If INR rises above 1.5, the patient needs hospitalisation and monitoring for acute liver failure. Never discharge a jaundiced patient without checking INR.
debrief.attendings_note:
This is a bread-and-butter case for Indian medicine — and yet students frequently overcomplicate it. Young man, just back from village, cluster of cases, contaminated water story, classic prodrome, now jaundice. This is hepatitis A until the blood test says otherwise. Order the Anti-HAV IgM and it will be positive.

The things I always check in viral hepatitis: the INR first, before anything else. If that INR is going up, I am admitting this patient immediately regardless of how well he looks. The liver can deteriorate very fast in fulminant hepatitis. This boy's INR is 1.2 and he is eating a little — he can go home with instructions.

Two other students from the same village trip also unwell — do not ignore that. That is a cluster. You need to inform the college medical officer and the district health authorities. This may be a local outbreak. Contact tracing and water testing may prevent 50 more cases.
debrief.india_context: HAV is the most common cause of acute viral hepatitis in Indian children and young adults. Post-monsoon outbreaks are classic — flooding contaminates drinking water sources. Tirunelveli, like many districts in Tamil Nadu, has periodic HAV outbreaks linked to water supply contamination. The HAV vaccine is not part of the national immunisation programme in India but is recommended for travellers and high-risk groups. HEV (Hepatitis E) must always be considered in the differential, especially in pregnant women where it carries 20–25% mortality.

related_cases.teaser: 28F, 32 weeks pregnant, jaundice post-flooding, anti-HEV IgM positive, worsening coagulopathy
related_cases.teaches: Hepatitis E in pregnancy — why it causes fulminant hepatic failure in pregnant women and carries 20% mortality

related_cases.teaser: 35M returning from rice fields during flood season, jaundice + fever + renal failure + conjunctival redness
related_cases.teaches: Leptospirosis with Weil's disease — how to distinguish from viral hepatitis using clinical features and specific serology
