cases.title: Fever From the Mines

patient.name: Ramkhelawan Yadav
patient.occupation: Construction worker, recently returned from a 3-month project in Chhattisgarh

cases.presentation: Ramkhelawan Yadav, 35 years old, is brought to casualty by his brother at 11pm. He has had high-grade fever for 5 days, treated at a local clinic with 'fever tablets' (Crocin and some antibiotics). Today his brother noticed he was behaving strangely — he could not recognise family members and was speaking incoherently. He is now barely responding. His brother mentions he has been working near forest areas in Chhattisgarh for 3 months.
cases.india_context: Chhattisgarh, Odisha, Jharkhand, and Madhya Pradesh account for over 40% of all malaria cases in India and nearly all deaths. Plasmodium falciparum dominates in forest and tribal regions. Migrant construction workers are at very high risk due to forest exposure, poor preventive measures, and delayed access to diagnosis. Cerebral malaria is defined by unarousable coma with P. falciparum parasitaemia and no other cause of coma.

history_cards.question: Exactly where in Chhattisgarh was he working and was there any jungle or forest nearby?
history_cards.answer: He was working at a mining construction site near Bastar district — surrounded by forest. He slept in a makeshift camp without mosquito nets.
history_cards.hint_value: Bastar district is one of the highest falciparum malaria endemic zones in India. Forest sleeping without nets = very high transmission risk.

history_cards.question: How has the fever been — continuous, or coming in episodes?
history_cards.answer: Initially high fever every day, then it seemed to come in cycles of 48–72 hours with shivering and then sweating. But for the last 2 days it has been continuous and very high.
history_cards.hint_value: Tertian/quartan periodicity is classic for P. vivax/malariae. Continuous high fever in falciparum = non-specific or cerebral involvement — falciparum rarely shows textbook periodicity

history_cards.question: Any other symptoms alongside the fever — dark urine, jaundice, decreased urination?
history_cards.answer: His brother says he noticed his urine was very dark, almost black, 2 days ago. His eyes look a bit yellow. He is not urinating much today.
history_cards.hint_value: Dark urine + jaundice + oliguria in falciparum = haemolysis + haemoglobinuria (blackwater fever) + early renal failure — severe malaria criteria met

history_cards.question: Was he taking any anti-malarial tablets prophylactically or was he treated for malaria at the site?
history_cards.answer: No prophylaxis at all. He was given some tablets by the local compounder at the site last week — he does not know what they were. He improved briefly then worsened.
history_cards.hint_value: Partial or suboptimal treatment (likely chloroquine for falciparum in a resistant area) allows parasitaemia to continue and facilitates progression to cerebral malaria

history_cards.question: Has he had any seizures or fits at home?
history_cards.answer: Yes — his brother says he had two convulsions yesterday. Each lasted about 2–3 minutes. He was confused after each one.
history_cards.hint_value: Seizures in a febrile patient from malaria-endemic area = cerebral malaria until proven otherwise. Generalised convulsions are a WHO criterion for severe malaria.

history_cards.question: Any known diabetes or hypertension?
history_cards.answer: No known illnesses. He has never been to a hospital before this year.
history_cards.hint_value: Low yield — no comorbidities to confound the picture. Noted as baseline.

findings.name: Temperature and HR
findings.result: Temp 40.1°C — high fever. HR 122 bpm — tachycardic.

findings.name: Blood Pressure and SpO2
findings.result: BP 90/60 mmHg — borderline hypotensive. SpO2 94% on room air.

findings.name: GCS
findings.result: E2V2M4 = 8/15 — unarousable coma. Moans to pain only.

findings.name: Pupils and Fundi
findings.result: Pupils equal and reactive 3mm bilaterally. Fundoscopy: Retinal whitening with macular pallor — malarial retinopathy. Papilloedema absent.

findings.name: Neck stiffness
findings.result: Mild neck stiffness — but Kernig's sign negative. No photophobia noted. Likely postictal or cerebral oedema rather than meningeal irritation.

tests.name: Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) for Malaria
tests.value: P. falciparum antigen (HRP-2): POSITIVE. P. vivax antigen: Negative.

tests.name: Peripheral Blood Smear (Thick and Thin)
tests.value: Thick smear: Numerous ring trophozoites of P. falciparum. Multiple parasites per RBC. Parasitaemia 8.2% (severe if > 2%). No gametocytes.

tests.name: CBC
tests.value: WBC 5.2 × 10⁹/L, Hb 7.1 g/dL (severe anaemia), Platelets 28 × 10⁹/L (severe thrombocytopaenia)

tests.name: Serum Bilirubin and LFTs
tests.value: Total bilirubin 6.8 mg/dL (indirect-predominant: 5.2 mg/dL). ALT 88 U/L (mildly elevated).

tests.name: Serum Creatinine and Urea
tests.value: Creatinine 3.8 mg/dL. Blood urea 88 mg/dL — acute kidney injury.

imaging_options.report:
No intracranial haemorrhage.
No space-occupying lesion.
Cerebral oedema: Mild diffuse cerebral oedema with effacement of sulci. No midline shift.
Ventricles: Normal calibre — no hydrocephalus.

Impression: Mild diffuse cerebral oedema. No structural lesion or haemorrhage. Findings consistent with cerebral malaria. Does not preclude lumbar puncture to exclude concurrent bacterial meningitis.

Spleen: Markedly enlarged — splenomegaly (span 19cm). Homogeneous echotexture. No splenic infarct.
Liver: Mildly enlarged. Slightly heterogeneous.
Kidneys: Mildly enlarged, increased cortical echogenicity — consistent with acute kidney injury.
Bladder: Small volume — oliguria confirmed.
No free fluid.

Impression: Massive splenomegaly consistent with malaria. Renal changes consistent with AKI.

Bilateral perihilar haze — early pulmonary oedema/ARDS.
Heart size normal.
No focal consolidation.

Impression: Early ARDS pattern in context of severe malaria — pulmonary oedema is a WHO criterion for severe malaria.

optimal_path.explanation: Fever + altered sensorium from a forest/tribal malaria zone = cerebral malaria until the smear says otherwise. First move: glucometer — hypoglycaemia is both a mimic AND a complication of severe malaria. Then RDT for immediate confirmation, followed by thick/thin smear for parasitaemia quantification. IV Artesunate should not wait for CT — treat empirically if smear/RDT confirms falciparum. CT head to exclude structural cause before LP if meningitis cannot be ruled out clinically.

debrief.pathophysiology: P. falciparum has unique ability to cause infected RBCs to cytoadhere to cerebral microvascular endothelium (via PfEMP-1 adhesin), causing mechanical obstruction and sequestration. Combined with endothelial activation, cytokine storm, and ischaemia, this causes cerebral oedema and neuronal dysfunction. Falciparum also sequesters in the placenta (dangerous in pregnancy) and can infect multiple RBCs per cell (double or triple ring forms). Hypoglycaemia occurs from high parasite glucose consumption AND quinine/artesunate stimulating insulin release.
debrief.classic_presentation: Fever + progressive altered sensorium + seizures in a migrant worker or traveller from a forest/tribal malaria-endemic zone. Severe anaemia, thrombocytopaenia, indirect hyperbilirubinaemia. Positive RDT and thick smear with high parasitaemia. Dark/black urine (haemoglobinuria in blackwater fever).
key_discriminators: Malaria zone history + fever + coma — do smear and RDT before any other workup
Indirect bilirubinaemia (haemolytic) + severe anaemia + thrombocytopaenia = haematological signature of falciparum
Multiple ring forms per RBC + 'appliqué' position + banana-shaped gametocytes = P. falciparum on smear
Hypoglycaemia in falciparum is both a cause and effect — check glucometer immediately
common_mimics: Bacterial meningitis — fever + altered sensorium + neck stiffness; CSF differentiates (neutrophilic pleocytosis vs clear); both can coexist
Viral encephalitis (JE) — endemic in same zones; mosquito-borne; fever + seizures + coma; smear negative; CSF lymphocytic
Typhoid encephalopathy — high Widal or blood culture; no parasitaemia; rose spots; relative bradycardia
debrief.treatment: IV Artesunate is the treatment of choice for severe malaria — superior to quinine in all trials. Dose: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24 hours, then once daily for 7 days. Switch to oral Artemether-Lumefantrine (Coartem/Falcigo) when patient can swallow. Correct hypoglycaemia immediately: 25% dextrose IV 50mL bolus. Blood transfusion if Hb < 7 g/dL. Manage AKI (fluid balance, dialysis if needed). Do NOT give steroids — increase mortality in cerebral malaria. Monitor blood glucose every 4 hours (artesunate also causes hypoglycaemia). Nurse in dark room, manage seizures with IV diazepam.
debrief.clinical_pearl: In any febrile patient with altered sensorium from a malaria-endemic area, do the glucometer first — takes 30 seconds and hypoglycaemia can mimic coma exactly. Then do RDT immediately. Never say 'we'll wait for the smear result' before initiating treatment in a comatose malaria patient — treat with IV Artesunate the moment RDT is positive.
debrief.attendings_note:
He came from Bastar. I saw that on the triage sheet and I told the resident — do malaria RDT now, before anything else. Forest zone + fever + confusion = falciparum malaria until I am proven wrong. We do not wait for CT, we do not wait for blood culture, we do not start empirical antibiotics for meningitis while we are waiting for the smear. The glucometer first, then the RDT, then Artesunate.

This man has all the features of severe malaria: coma, seizures, Hb 7, platelets 28, creatinine 3.8, bilirubin 6.8, glucose 48, lactate 6. By WHO criteria he has cerebral malaria, severe anaemia, AKI, hypoglycaemia, and lactic acidosis — that is five criteria for severe disease. The mortality in cerebral malaria without treatment within a few hours is very high.

Do not give steroids. I know it is tempting in a comatose patient with cerebral oedema — but studies showed steroids increase mortality in cerebral malaria. And do not give chloroquine — most falciparum in India is chloroquine resistant. Artesunate only.
debrief.india_context: India's malaria burden is concentrated in 5 high-burden states: Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Odisha, Madhya Pradesh, and West Bengal. P. falciparum dominates in these forested zones and has widespread chloroquine resistance. Migrant construction workers moving from these states to cities are the primary vector for introducing malaria to urban centres. Under the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP), Rapid Diagnostic Tests and IV Artesunate are available free at government hospitals.

related_cases.teaches: Malaria in pregnancy — why it is more dangerous, preferential placental sequestration, and treatment differences

related_cases.teaser: 14M, Uttar Pradesh, fever + seizures + coma, smear negative, CSF shows lymphocytes and low glucose
related_cases.teaches: Japanese Encephalitis — same endemic belt, same presentation, differentiated by serology and negative malaria smear

related_cases.label: Same chief complaint, different Plasmodium
related_cases.teaser: 32M returning from Rajasthan, cyclic fever every 48 hours, chills, sweating, anaemia, smear positive P. vivax
related_cases.teaches: P. vivax malaria — benign vs falciparum differences, chloroquine-sensitive, need for primaquine to clear hypnozoites

cases.title: More Than Just a Viral Fever
cases.presentation: Siddharth Rajan, 16 years old, is brought by his mother to a private hospital in Chennai. He has had high fever for 5 days — his local paediatrician started him on antibiotics for 'viral fever with possible typhoid' 3 days ago. Today his mother is very worried: the fever has reduced slightly but he has severe abdominal pain, has vomited twice, and she noticed small reddish spots appearing on his forearms. He looks pale and unwell.
cases.india_context: India reports approximately 200,000 dengue cases annually (significant under-reporting). Tamil Nadu, particularly Chennai, is hyperendemic with year-round transmission peaking October–November. Dengue is a notifiable disease under the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme. The most dangerous mistake is discharging a dengue patient without recognising warning signs — which include abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, rapid breathing, bleeding, and lethargy.

history_cards.question: Describe the fever pattern over 5 days and how he looks today compared to day 1.
history_cards.answer: First 2–3 days: very high fever (39.5–40°C), severe headache, body ache. Day 4: fever came down slightly — family thought he was recovering. Day 5 today: still feverish, but now abdominal pain, vomiting, and he is more lethargic than before.
history_cards.hint_value: The classic dengue 'saddleback' fever pattern: high fever days 1–3, brief defervescence, then deterioration on day 4–5 with warning signs. This is the most dangerous phase — when leaking starts.

history_cards.question: Any bleeding — from the nose, gums, or elsewhere?
history_cards.answer: He had a small nosebleed yesterday that stopped on its own. His mother noticed he is bruising easily — she bumped his arm lightly and it left a big bruise.
history_cards.hint_value: Mucosal bleeding + easy bruising = thrombocytopaenia-related bleeding — warning sign of progression to DHF

history_cards.question: Any other cases of dengue in the neighbourhood or school recently?
history_cards.answer: Yes — three of his classmates had dengue in the past month. The area had waterlogging after heavy rains. There is a construction site nearby with stagnant water.
history_cards.hint_value: Cluster of Aedes aegypti-transmitted dengue in a waterlogged urban area during monsoon is a classic epidemiological picture

history_cards.question: Has anyone given him aspirin or ibuprofen for the fever?
history_cards.answer: The local doctor initially gave him Combiflam (ibuprofen + paracetamol). He took it for 2 days before coming here.
history_cards.hint_value: NSAIDs in dengue are dangerous — ibuprofen and aspirin inhibit platelet function and can precipitate serious bleeding. Only paracetamol is safe in dengue.

history_cards.question: Any difficulty breathing or chest pain?
history_cards.answer: No chest pain. But his breathing seems a bit faster than usual to his mother. He has not measured it.
history_cards.hint_value: Increased respiratory rate can indicate pleural effusion or pulmonary oedema from plasma leakage — an important examination finding to elicit

history_cards.question: What is his urine output like over the past 24 hours?
history_cards.answer: His mother is not sure — he has not been going to the bathroom much today. Maybe twice since morning.
history_cards.hint_value: Reduced urine output = decreased perfusion from plasma leakage / early dengue shock. Monitor fluid status carefully.

findings.name: Heart Rate and BP
findings.result: HR 108 bpm. BP 102/80 mmHg. Pulse pressure NARROW: 22 mmHg (normal > 40 mmHg) — early shock indicator.

findings.result: Temp 38.3°C. SpO2 96% on room air. RR 22/min.

findings.result: Capillary refill 3 seconds (prolonged). Skin cool and clammy peripherally.

findings.result: Positive: > 20 petechiae per square inch after 5 minutes BP cuff inflation at mean arterial pressure.

imaging_options.report:
Pleural effusion: Right-sided pleural effusion (small — 2cm depth). Ascites: Small perihepatically and perisplenic — plasma leakage.
Gallbladder: Wall oedema (GB wall 6mm — thickened). Pericholecystic fluid.
Spleen: Mildly enlarged (13cm).
Liver: Normal size, mildly heterogeneous.

Impression: Pleural effusion + ascites + GB wall oedema = third-spacing from plasma leakage. Hallmark of dengue haemorrhagic fever / dengue with warning signs.

Right basal haziness — small right pleural effusion.
Left lung: Clear.
Heart size: Upper normal.
No consolidation.

Impression: Small right pleural effusion consistent with plasma leakage in dengue. No pneumonia.

Bilateral pleural effusions (right larger than left) — increased from 24 hours ago.
No consolidation. No pulmonary oedema on CXR.

Impression: Expanding pleural effusions during critical phase of dengue — monitor respiratory status closely. If SpO2 falling, reassess for dengue shock syndrome.

optimal_path.explanation: Monsoon season + Chennai + 5-day fever + thrombocytopaenia in a teenager = dengue until proven otherwise. The critical insight is recognising WARNING SIGNS on day 4–5: abdominal pain, vomiting, bleeding, rising haematocrit, narrow pulse pressure. NS1 antigen confirms active dengue. Rising haematocrit + falling platelets + hypoalbuminaemia = plasma leakage confirmed. USG showing pleural effusion + ascites = critical phase. Admit and monitor 4-hourly haematocrit.

debrief.pathophysiology: Dengue is caused by DENV (4 serotypes) transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquito. Primary infection produces immunity to that serotype. Secondary infection with a different serotype causes antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) — cross-reactive antibodies bind but do not neutralise new serotype, instead facilitating greater viral entry into monocytes. This triggers massive cytokine release, endothelial injury, and plasma leakage. Plasma leaks from intravascular to third spaces (pleura, peritoneum) causing haemoconcentration (rising haematocrit) and hypovolaemia.
debrief.classic_presentation: Sudden high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, arthralgia ('breakbone fever'). Rash — maculopapular or petechial. Thrombocytopaenia and leucopaenia. On day 4–5, defervescence with WARNING SIGNS: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, rapid breathing, bleeding, liver enlargement, restlessness or lethargy. Without hospitalisation, can progress to dengue shock syndrome (narrow pulse pressure or hypotension).
key_discriminators: Rising haematocrit (>20% from baseline) = plasma leakage = dengue haemorrhagic fever criterion
Narrow pulse pressure < 20 mmHg = early dengue shock — do not confuse as 'improving' because fever came down
GB wall oedema + pleural effusion + ascites on USG = third-spacing from plasma leakage
Warning signs on day 4–5 after fever defervescence = most dangerous window
common_mimics: Typhoid fever — 'step-ladder' fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, Widal titre positive, responds to antibiotics
Scrub typhus — eschar at bite site, lymphadenopathy, Weil-Felix positive, responds to doxycycline
ITP — isolated thrombocytopaenia without fever or systemic illness, no serological evidence of infection
debrief.treatment: STOP NSAIDs immediately — only paracetamol for fever. Admit for monitoring. IV fluids: Ringer's Lactate, NOT saline. Do NOT over-hydrate — can worsen third-spacing and cause pulmonary oedema. Titrate fluids to haematocrit every 4–6 hours. Platelet transfusion: only if < 10,000 or active significant bleeding — NOT prophylactically for low counts. Monitor for shock: pulse pressure, capillary refill, urine output. Dengue shock: IV crystalloid bolus 10–20 mL/kg over 15 minutes. No steroids, no antibiotics (unless co-infection proven).
debrief.clinical_pearl: Haematocrit rise of 20% or more from baseline is the most sensitive indicator of plasma leakage and imminent dengue shock. A patient whose fever comes down on day 4–5 but whose haematocrit is rising is getting worse, not better. The defervescence phase is the most dangerous phase of dengue — not the febrile phase. This counter-intuitive fact leads to many delayed recognitions of shock.
debrief.attendings_note:
The local doctor gave Combiflam and called it viral fever. That is the one medication you must never give in dengue — ibuprofen and aspirin further inhibit platelets that are already being destroyed. By the time I saw him, his platelets were at 32 and he had a nosebleed. The Combiflam did not cause the dengue but it made the bleeding worse.

Now look at the pulse pressure. The boy has BP 102/80 — the nurses recorded it as 'normal BP.' But the pulse pressure is 22 — that is abnormal. A pulse pressure below 20 means early shock. His body is compensating by raising diastolic. This is the subtlest and most important vital sign in dengue. I want you to calculate pulse pressure in every dengue patient, every time you check BP.

The parents thought he was getting better because the fever was coming down on day 4. This is the most dangerous misconception in dengue. Tell every dengue patient and family: defervescence is not recovery — it is the beginning of the critical phase. Day 4–6 is when we watch the most closely.
debrief.india_context: Chennai is hyperendemic for dengue with serotypes 1–4 all circulating. The Aedes aegypti mosquito breeds in clean stagnant water — coolers, flower pots, construction sites, water tanks. Post-monsoon October–November is the peak season. All dengue cases must be notified to NVBDCP. Under-reporting is massive — official figures represent roughly 10% of actual cases. Platelet transfusion is frequently overused in India — evidence does not support transfusion unless platelets < 10,000 or significant active bleeding.
